blurb: A bulb succulent with lance-shaped leaves silvered on top and spotted dark green, sitting on purple, above-ground bulbs. It clusters into a colony — 'socialis' — and is tougher than it looks.

care.light: Bright light, including some gentle direct sun, keeps the silver-and-green mottling and purple bulbs vivid; in shade the colours fade and leaves stretch.
care.water: Water when it's in active growth and the soil dries; ease right off as it rests. The bulbs sit proud of the soil and rot if buried or kept wet.
care.soil: A gritty, free-draining mix with the bulbs sitting on top.
care.temperature: Room temperature; keep above ~40°F and drier when cool.
care.dormancy: A warm-season grower that rests in winter and may drop some leaves — keep it barely watered then until growth resumes.
care.feeding: A dilute feed once or twice during active growth.
care.potting: It clumps quickly into a mat of bulbs; repot or divide every couple of years, leaving the bulbs proud.
care.propagation: Simply separate offset bulbs from the clump — they establish fast.
care.bloom: Slender spikes of small greenish-white bell flowers on pink stems, mostly in spring.
care.troubles: Overwatering rots the bulbs; otherwise largely pest-free, with the odd mealybug — watch also for aphids, spider mites and scale.
care.toxicity: Toxic to cats and dogs if eaten — it contains oxalic acid, and ingestion can cause vomiting and worse — so keep it out of reach; the sap can also irritate skin.

sources.title: Ledebouria socialis (Silver Squill) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
